content.text: Every generation of city-builders inherits a particular set of instruments. Our predecessors planned Seoul with drafting tables, cadastral maps, and the patient accumulation of paper masterplans. Today the instruments are changing faster than at any moment in the seventy years since the Seoul Institute's forerunners first began to study this city systematically. Big data, machine learning, urban digital twins, and now AI agents are no longer laboratory curiosities; they are becoming the everyday working tools of the planner. This book is published because the Seoul Institute believes that such a moment of transition deserves to be documented honestly, while it is still unfolding, by the practitioners living inside it.

Seoul occupies an unusual position in the global urban order. It is a megacity of ten million that grew, within a single lifetime, from the rubble of war to one of the most digitally connected metropolitan regions on earth. That trajectory gives Seoul both a story worth telling and an obligation. We have learned a great deal — about rapid urbanization, about e-government, about open data, and lately about putting artificial intelligence to work in the service of public planning. A leading global city does not hoard such knowledge. It shares method, not merely outcomes, so that planners in other world cities and in rapidly growing cities of the developing world can adapt what is useful and discard what is not.

But sharing is only half of what this book argues for. The harder, and in some ways more original, claim is that the cities of the twenty-first century must also cooperate differently. The age of the one-off study tour and the annual ceremonial forum is passing. What megacities need now is standing, research-driven cooperation among their think tanks — durable institutional relationships through which data, models, and policy lessons travel continuously rather than episodically. The Seoul Institute has spent the past several years building exactly these kinds of relationships, from multilateral megacity networks to bilateral research partnerships and open geospatial collaborations.

With this volume the Seoul Institute inaugurates its Global Urban Research book series, an English-language line of works intended to carry Korean planning experience into international scholarly and policy conversation. We have deliberately modeled this first book on UN-Habitat's well-loved Urban Planning for City Leaders and its companion Urban Planning for Growing Cities: it is written to be read by busy decision-makers, grounded in concrete projects rather than abstraction, and candid about what has not yet worked. I commend it to mayors, planners, researchers, and students alike, and I thank the authors and our international partners whose joint work fills these pages.

content.en: Introduction: Planning the Global City with AI

content.text: A planner once needed a pencil, a map, and a meeting room. She still needs all three. But increasingly she also needs a model that can simulate the accessibility of every neighborhood in the city before lunch, a digital twin that lets her walk a proposed street before a single curb is poured, and an archive that answers questions rather than merely storing documents. This book is about what happens to the practice of urban planning when those new instruments arrive — and about what happens to the relationships between cities when planning becomes a data-intensive, continuously cooperative enterprise.

Our central argument is that two transitions are reshaping how a global city plans itself, and that they are happening at the same time. The first is technological. For a generation, planning's frontier was e-government: digitizing permits, putting maps online, opening data portals. That frontier has now moved. The leading edge today runs through big data on how people actually move and live, through machine-learning models that detect patterns no zoning code anticipated, through urban digital twins that fuse three-dimensional city models with live sensor feeds, and through AI agents that can stand in for citizens in policy simulations. The paper masterplan does not disappear, but it becomes the visible output of a far more dynamic computational substrate beneath it.

The second transition is relational. As planning becomes data-driven, the unit of useful exchange between cities changes. It is no longer enough for a delegation to visit, photograph a good bus lane, and fly home. What travels well in a data age is method: the model specification, the data schema, the evaluation framework, the failure honestly reported. Transmitting these requires more than a forum once a year; it requires standing institutional cooperation between the research bodies that megacities maintain — their think tanks, their planning institutes, their geospatial agencies. The book calls this shift the move from study tours to research diplomacy, and it treats the two transitions as inseparable: the technological transition is what makes the relational one both possible and necessary.

Seoul, through the Seoul Institute, is experimenting at the intersection of both transitions, and this book is told through that institution's projects from 2023 to 2026. These are not hypotheticals. They include an AI-based living-zone planning system that combines community detection, accessibility modeling, and optimization; a fifteen-minute-city analysis recast as a data problem; the S-Map urban digital twin descended from earlier Virtual Seoul efforts; experiments with citizen personas and AI agents for policy simulation; a Korea–Canada study of autonomous sidewalk robots; the globalization of the Seoul Policy Archive into executable knowledge; and the construction of multilateral and bilateral research networks among megacities. The book's authority rests on the fact that it reports work actually attempted, with its results and its rough edges intact.

Who is this book for? Primarily for policymakers and planners in world cities and in the fast-growing cities of the developing world — the same readership UN-Habitat's city-leader guides address. We have written for the intelligent generalist, not the specialist alone. A mayor's advisor should be able to read a chapter and grasp what a method does, what it costs, and what it demands of an institution, without first earning a degree in data science. Readers who want technical depth will find enough signposting to go further; readers who want strategy can read the opening and closing of each chapter and the two part-introductions and come away with the argument intact. The book can be read straight through, but each chapter is also designed to stand on its own as a case.

The architecture follows the two transitions directly. Part 1, AI and Data Methods for Planning, moves from foundations to frontier: from the machine-learning city and the legacy of planning information systems and e-government, through the urban digital twin and the fifteen-minute city as a data problem, to AI-based living-zone planning and finally to citizen personas and AI agents. Part 2, International Urban Research Cooperation, turns to the relational transition: digital twins and autonomous mobility across the Korea–Canada partnership, the globalization of the Seoul Policy Archive, the multilateral MeTTA megacity-network model, the bilateral Seoul–Shanghai research network, open geospatial cooperation as a form of urban diplomacy, and the institutionalization of all of it. Method first, then the networks through which method travels.

A final word on tone. It would be easy to write a triumphant book about a digitally advanced city, and easy to write a cynical one about technology's broken promises. We have tried to do neither. Seoul's experiments have produced real, measurable gains, and we report them plainly because false modesty helps no one trying to learn from them. But every method in this book also has limits — in data quality, in equity, in the risk of letting a model quietly displace human judgment, in the fragility of cooperation that depends on a few committed individuals. We name those limits as carefully as we name the successes. A book meant to be useful to other cities owes them an honest ledger, not a brochure.

chapters.label: 1장
chapters.partname: Part 1 · AI and Data Methods for Planning
chapters.titleEN: The Machine-Learning City: Foundations of AI in Urban Planning

content.text: "The city is the most complex artefact humanity has ever built — and for the first time we have instruments fine enough to read it."

For most of the twentieth century, urban planners worked with thin data and thick judgement. A master plan rested on a decennial census, a handful of cordon traffic counts, and the intuition of experienced officials. The map was static, the survey was expensive, and the future was projected by drawing trend lines forward. That world is ending. A contemporary city generates a continuous exhaust of digital traces — mobile-phone handovers, transit-card taps, sensor readings, satellite passes, social-media posts, administrative transactions — at a volume no clerk could ever tabulate. The question is no longer whether data exist, but whether the profession can turn that flood into better decisions about the shared physical environment.

This chapter lays the conceptual foundation for the chapters that follow. It does three things. First, it frames the city as a complex adaptive system — the premise that makes machine learning relevant to planning rather than a mere accounting convenience. Second, it offers a working taxonomy of the data sources and methods now available to planners, so a reader can locate any specific tool within a larger map. Third, it argues a position that runs through the whole book: artificial intelligence is most valuable when it augments the judgement of the planner and the deliberation of the public, and most dangerous when it is asked to replace them. The audience is global: a planner in a rapidly urbanising city does not need Seoul's budget to begin, but a clear mental model of what these instruments can and cannot do — which is what this chapter supplies.

The starting principle is that a city is a complex adaptive system, not a machine to be optimised in one pass. Its form emerges from millions of locally rational decisions — where to live, where to open a shop, which route to take — that aggregate into patterns no single actor designed. The scholarship gathered in works such as The Machine Learning City shows that urban structure exhibits the signatures of complexity: fractal geometry in the way settlement fills space, power-law distributions in city sizes and street-segment lengths, and emergent order in network topology. This matters for method. If urban patterns are emergent, then top-down equations that assume equilibrium will miss the dynamics that actually shape the city; approaches that learn patterns from observed behaviour, or that simulate behaviour from the bottom up, are a better epistemological fit.

On that foundation sits a taxonomy of data. Five families are now routinely available. Administrative data — building registers, parcel records, permits, tax rolls — are the oldest and most authoritative, structured by law and tied to legal decisions. Sensor and IoT data — traffic detectors, air-quality monitors, smart streetlights, water meters — capture the real-time state of infrastructure. Mobile and telecommunications data — anonymised, aggregated handset locations from mobile-network operators — reveal where people are and how they move at a granularity censuses cannot reach. Satellite and remote-sensing data — optical and radar imagery, night-time lights, land-surface temperature — allow even data-poor cities to map growth, informal settlement, and green cover without a single field survey. Crowdsourced and volunteered data — OpenStreetMap, geolocated social media, civic-complaint apps — add texture and citizen voice. Each family has a characteristic bias, and a competent planner reads them together rather than trusting any one alone.

Paired with the data is a taxonomy of methods. Supervised machine learning learns a mapping from labelled examples — predicting land-use change, housing price, or flood risk from past cases. Unsupervised learning finds structure without labels — clustering neighbourhoods by morphological type, or detecting anomalous growth. Network science treats the street system, the transit graph, and social ties as networks, computing centrality, accessibility, and community structure; the analysis of street networks is one of the field's most mature applications. Simulation — cellular automata for land-use change and agent-based models for travel, segregation, or evacuation — lets planners run "what-if" experiments on a digital city before committing concrete in the real one. And generative AI — large language and image-and-design models — can now draft scenarios, summarise public comment, and produce design variants for human review. These are not rival schools; they are a toolkit, and good practice combines them.

The organising principle that ties the toolkit to public purpose is augmentation, not automation. Machine learning is exceptionally good at perception and prediction — seeing patterns in high-dimensional data and extrapolating them. It is poor at judgement under contested values, which is the heart of planning: deciding whose interests prevail, how to weigh growth against equity, what kind of city a community wants to become. A model can tell a planner that a corridor is likely to gentrify; it cannot decide whether that is acceptable. The right division of labour gives the machine the perception-and-prediction work and reserves the value-laden decision for accountable humans and democratic process. AI in planning should expand the space of options a planner can consider and the evidence a community can weigh, not narrow either down to a number presented as destiny.

Finally, the foundations include limits and ethics as a design constraint, not an afterthought. Models inherit the biases of their training data: if historical investment skipped poor districts, a model trained on it will recommend skipping them again. Complex models are often opaque — a "black box" whose reasoning a planner cannot explain to a resident or a court, which is incompatible with the duty to give reasons for public decisions. And the data carry profound privacy stakes; mobility traces, even aggregated, can re-identify individuals if handled carelessly. A city that adopts these tools must adopt, in the same motion, a data-governance regime: legal basis, anonymisation standards, model documentation, human review of consequential decisions, and an audit trail.

Seoul illustrates the principles above not as a finished utopia but as a working laboratory. Three uses of data and machine learning show the range — from mobility analytics that reshaped a public service, to a research programme that institutionalises AI for planning, to an emerging use of generative AI in design review.

content.kr: 심야버스: 모바일 빅데이터 기반 이동 분석
content.en: Owl Bus: Mobile Big-Data Mobility Analysis

content.text: **Background & context.** In the early 2010s Seoul faced a familiar problem with a novel solution. Late-night workers — cleaners, service staff, shift workers — were poorly served after the subway closed, but the city had no reliable picture of where they travelled at night. Conventional surveys were too sparse and too slow to capture nocturnal demand. The Seoul Metropolitan Government partnered with the mobile-network operator KT to use anonymised, aggregated late-night call-and-text records as a proxy for the origins and destinations of people moving across the city between midnight and 5 a.m.

**Policy content & implementation.** Roughly three billion late-night communication records were aggregated to a grid and overlaid with registered-address data to estimate flows. Clustering and density analysis identified the corridors of greatest demand, and these patterns — not political negotiation alone — were used to draw the routes of the new "Owl Bus" (Night Owl) network. The analysis converted an opaque social need into a spatial pattern that planners could act on, and the routes were iterated as ridership data accumulated. The project became one of the most cited early examples of big-data-driven public policy in any city.

**Outcomes & limitations.** The Owl Bus became a durable, popular service and a flagship of Seoul's data governance, demonstrating that aggregated telecommunications data could answer a question no survey could. Its limits are instructive. Call-and-text records proxy presence imperfectly and skew by who uses their phone at night; the method located demand but could not by itself adjudicate equity or cost. And the privacy guarantee rested entirely on aggregation and the absence of personal identifiers — a discipline that must be enforced, not assumed.

**Implications for global and developing cities.** The transferable lesson is that a single, well-scoped question — "where do people move at night?" — turned a commercial data exhaust into a public good at modest cost. Many cities already have a mobile-operator that holds equivalent data; the binding constraints are a data-sharing agreement, an anonymisation standard, and the analytical capacity to act on the result. A developing city need not build a smart-city platform to start; it can start with one question that matters.

content.en: The AI Urban-Planning Support R&D Programme

content.text: **Background & context.** As data accumulated, Seoul and Korean research institutions recognised that ad-hoc analytics would not institutionalise themselves. A research-and-development programme was needed to turn machine-learning techniques into reusable planning instruments — for delineating living zones, measuring accessibility, and supporting facility-location and re-allocation decisions — and to embed them in the workflow of public planners rather than leaving them in academic papers.

**Policy content & implementation.** The programme combines methods drawn from network science and operations research: community detection to delineate functional neighbourhoods, two-step floating catchment methods to measure access to facilities, and optimisation to propose where under-served services should be added or relocated. Crucially, the outputs are designed as decision-support — maps, indicators, and ranked options that a planner interrogates — rather than as automated verdicts. The work has been piloted on real Korean cities and tied to keynote arguments, such as J.Y. Choi's "Digital Technology for Planning Mega Cities," that frame AI as a partner to planning creativity.

**Outcomes & limitations.** The programme's value is that it converts one-off studies into transferable tools and trains a cadre of planners fluent in them. The limitation is the perennial gap between a research prototype and an operational system: maintenance, data refresh, and integration with statutory information systems (the subject of Chapter 2) are harder than the modelling. An optimisation that proposes a facility location is only as legitimate as the public process that ratifies it; the model informs the decision, it does not own it.

**Implications for global and developing cities.** The model here is institutional, not merely technical: pair a city with a research partner, target a recurring planning task, and deliver a reusable tool plus the training to wield it. This is a more durable path than buying a one-off consultancy study, and it builds local capacity — the scarcest resource in most cities — rather than renting it.

content.kr: 생성형 AI 기반 가로·경관 설계 검토
content.en: Generative AI for Street and Design Review

content.text: **Background & context.** Design review — judging whether a proposed building or streetscape fits its context — is labour-intensive, subjective, and slow. As generative AI matured, Seoul's planning research community began testing whether image-generation and large-language models could accelerate the production and assessment of design alternatives, particularly for street and public-realm design where many small decisions accumulate into the experience of a neighbourhood.

**Policy content & implementation.** In exploratory use, generative models produce rapid visual variants of a street section or façade for reviewers to compare, while language models summarise lengthy public comments and statutory guidance so committees can focus on substance. These tools sit upstream of decision: they widen the option set and compress the drudgery, leaving the design judgement — and the legal determination — to human reviewers and the urban design committee.

**Outcomes & limitations.** Early results suggest real gains in speed and in the breadth of options considered, which is exactly the augmentation thesis in practice. The limitations are sharp: generative models can produce plausible but contextually wrong imagery, they encode the aesthetic biases of their training data, and their outputs carry no statutory authority. Used uncritically, they could homogenise design or launder bias behind a veneer of objectivity. They are best treated as a sketchbook, not a judge.

**Implications for global and developing cities.** Generative AI lowers the threshold for visualising and deliberating design, which is valuable where design expertise is scarce. But the governance lesson dominates the technical one: any city adopting these tools should mandate human authorship of decisions, disclosure that AI was used, and review for bias and contextual error. The tool democratises the production of options; the city must still own the choice among them.

Seen together, Seoul's experiences confirm the chapter's thesis. The machine-learning city is not one in which algorithms govern; it is one in which planners and citizens see further, faster, and more fairly because they are equipped with better instruments. The Owl Bus showed perception — making an invisible demand visible. The R&D programme showed institutionalisation — turning a technique into a durable tool and a trained workforce. The design-review experiments show the frontier — generative AI as a sketchbook for human judgement. In every case the value came from a clear question and a disciplined division of labour between machine and human.

The complement to capability is restraint. Every advance in this chapter carries a matching obligation: bias must be tested for, opacity must be mitigated with explanation, and data must be governed with a legal basis and an audit trail. A city that adopts the tools without the governance does not become smarter; it becomes faster at being wrong, and less able to explain itself to its residents. Capability and accountability must be built in the same motion.

For a growing city deciding where to begin, the path is reassuringly concrete. Start with one question that matters and the data you already have; build the institutional muscle to act on the answer; and write the rules of governance before, not after, the tools scale. The following takeaways distil this into practice.

1. Treat the city as a complex adaptive system. Prefer methods that learn from observed behaviour or simulate it from the bottom up over equilibrium models that assume away the dynamics planning must manage.

2. Read the data families together. Administrative, sensor, mobile, satellite, and crowdsourced data each carry a characteristic bias; triangulating them is more reliable than trusting any single source.
3. Augment, do not automate. Assign perception and prediction to the machine and reserve contested, value-laden decisions for accountable humans and democratic process.
4. Build governance in the same motion as capability. Mandate a legal basis, anonymisation, model documentation, bias testing, and human review before consequential use, not after.
5. Start small and build capacity. One well-scoped question on data you already hold, delivered as a reusable tool with trained staff, beats a one-off study or an unused platform.

chapters.label: 2장
chapters.partname: Part 1 · AI and Data Methods for Planning
chapters.titleEN: Urban Planning Information Systems and e-Government

content.text: "A city is built from the accumulation of decisions, and those decisions are made on top of information. The quality of urban planning is the quality of the information that supports it."

Rational planning is possible only when the current condition of a site, its surroundings, the history of past decisions, the relevant laws and higher-order plans, and the views of residents can be gathered and compared. In the late 1990s, as the development of Gangnam and large-scale urban redevelopment ran in parallel, Seoul's demand for planning administration exploded — yet the vast body of documents and drawings lay scattered across paper and filing cabinets, delaying both decisions and the handling of citizen requests. The digital information system emerged as the answer to that problem.

This chapter examines the informational foundation of urban planning through a three-layer model. The first layer is e-government — the institutional and technical base on which any information system runs, which has evolved from administrative computerisation through e-government and digital government to today's digital platform government. The second is the Urban Planning Information System (UPIS) — a Planning Support System (PSS) that supports the core planning tasks of drafting, deliberation, decision, public notice, and updating. The third is urban information itself: the three-dimensional digital twin that reproduces spatial reality so it can be analysed and simulated (treated fully in Chapter 3). The decisive point is that the three layers are not independent. If e-government's data governance is weak, the UPIS becomes inaccurate; if the UPIS's data are inaccurate, the digital twin produces analyses that are plausible but wrong. The information system must be designed as one connected whole.

The first layer, e-government, is a means of government reform through information technology. The concept appeared in the United States in 1993 as 'reinventing government through information technology'; Korea selected 'realising a small but efficient e-government' as the first of its ten national informatisation tasks in 1996. From 1980s administrative computerisation, through 2000s e-government, to the opening of public data after 2010, Korea has held a top rank in the UN e-Government surveys. Yet its scores on government effectiveness, national competitiveness, and corruption perception have lagged well behind its digital-government rank — a gap that suggests early e-government too often digitised existing procedures without redesigning the underlying process, and so did not translate into governing capacity. The lesson is foundational: digitising a broken workflow yields a faster broken workflow.

The response to that gap is digital platform government. Its core is a change in the role of the state: from 'government as a service' (GaaS), a monopoly supplier delivering services in one direction, to 'government as a platform' (GaaP), on which the private sector and citizens build innovation. Data shift from 'selective opening after creation' to 'full opening from the moment of creation,' and channels expand from Web 2.0 to the all-device, metaverse-capable Web 3.0. This is not merely rhetorical: it changes who may build value on public data, and therefore how much value the data generate.

Yet the transition is a problem of governance before it is a problem of technology. Participation and transparency become real only when 'digital governance' — the maturation of e-government — meshes with 'network governance,' cooperation among stakeholders. Three international cases are cited as standards: Estonia's X-Road, a distributed data-exchange backbone that lets agencies share data without a central monolith; Singapore's CORENET and 'Moments of Life,' delivering one-stop construction administration and life-event services; and the United Kingdom's GOV.UK and G-Cloud, a single citizen portal paired with cloud procurement. Their common features are decisive: strong legal groundwork, cooperative governance, and standardised digital infrastructure. On that base, urban informatisation takes cloud migration, data governance, and data openness as its core tasks.

The second layer, the UPIS, is widely misunderstood as a map-search service that displays planning decisions on a screen. It is not. A UPIS is a Planning Support System that connects drafting, consultation, deliberation, decision, public notice, citizen disclosure, and subsequent updating into a single flow of data — supporting both the official's decision-making and the citizen's right to know. Its legal basis lies in land-use regulation and national planning law, given concrete form in Korea by the 2012 regulation on the construction and operation of urban planning information systems. Structurally it has three tiers: a data tier that stores source records; an analysis-and-task tier that supports planning work such as overlay queries, conflict checks, and the preparation of deliberation materials; and a disclosure-and-communication tier that conveys information to citizens through portals, alerts, and APIs.

The quality of a UPIS is determined not by the polish of its interface but by the completeness of its data. If a single parcel shows inconsistent information across use-zone, district-unit plan, height limit, facility, and redevelopment-area layers, users stop trusting the system. Data management must therefore be understood not as a one-time 'input' but as life-cycle management of the plan: a provisional layer is created at drafting, changes are recorded through inspection and deliberation, a confirmed layer is linked to the original notice after promulgation, and even when a plan is amended or abolished the historical record is preserved. Performance, likewise, must be judged not by 'number of visitors' but by the accuracy, completeness, and timeliness of the data, and by efficiency, accessibility, interoperability, and accountability.

The third layer, the digital twin, reproduces spatial reality so that phenomena invisible in two dimensions — wind, sunlight, view, flooding — can be analysed and simulated; it is developed in full in Chapter 3. A two-dimensional system answers 'what is where'; a three-dimensional twin answers 'what effect does it have on space.' The twin's analyses are only as trustworthy as the planning data the UPIS feeds it, which is only as trustworthy as the e-government governance beneath both — which is why the three layers must be designed together, not procured separately and stitched up afterward.

Seoul has operated its own urban planning information system since the mid-2000s, making it one of the cities with the longest experience of planning informatisation. From its accumulated practice we draw three cases that span the model's layers: S-UPIS, which integrated scattered planning information (the UPIS layer); the building-and-housing comprehensive information system, which repaired the e-government foundation; and Virtual Seoul / S-Map, the digital twin built on top (the urban-information layer). The selection criterion was to represent the layers evenly, to be immediately referenceable by a city beginning its own informatisation, and to show successes and limits together.

**Background & context.** In the late 1990s, large-scale urban development caused administrative demand to surge, while the documents and drawings generated at each stage lay scattered, delaying decisions. Planning matters in Seoul unfold over ten to twenty years; paper files and the hand-over between successive officials could not reliably manage the history of changes or the grounds for past decisions. As GIS technology matured and made it possible to link spatial information with maps and attributes, the construction of S-UPIS began in 1999 under the city's geographic-information division.

**Policy content & implementation.** The build ran for roughly six years (1999–2004) at a cost of about 5.2 billion won, structuring the drafting-decision-notice information of urban management plans, use-zones and districts, planning facilities, district-unit plans, and redevelopment projects into spatial layers with attributes. After a pilot in five districts, full operation began across Seoul and its 25 districts in March 2006. Full internal operation in 2010 and a citizen-facing urban-planning portal in 2011 followed, and a web-based upgrade in 2017–2020 extended the system to Seoul-specific tasks such as baseline surveys, public contributions, and public land resources.

**Outcomes & limitations.** Coupling the drafting-to-decision procedure with GIS drawings let the city's headquarters (some 740 staff) and the autonomous districts (about 1,087 staff) review the same information, and accumulating the history of long-running projects as data created a basis for administrative continuity. Yet utilisation outside drafting, environmental review, and disaster-vulnerability analysis remains low, and data other than drafting records are not systematically updated after registration. As the organisation expanded far beyond the original target user base, functions did not keep pace with policy diversification; and because data production was not anyone's statutory duty, continuity and consistency of upkeep suffered.

**Implications for global and developing cities.** Informatisation is not 'a project to build a website.' The decisive move is to put the drafting-decision-notice-update administrative flow into the system, to manage spatial data and original documents together, and to make the internal work portal and the citizen portal share one database. The harder truth is that a system is more difficult to 'maintain' than to 'build': data-quality management must be made a standing, statutory administrative duty, with dedicated staff and budget designed in from the start, not bolted on later.

content.en: The Building & Housing Comprehensive Information System

content.text: **Background & context.** For a UPIS to run on trustworthy data, the building-and-housing administrative information that underpins it must first be put in order. That information was scattered across systems built law-by-law and task-by-task — permitting in one, transactions in another, redevelopment in a third, 3D spatial information in S-Map — and the data produced by housing-bureau divisions alone reached more than a thousand managed items, making integrated management nearly impossible. Seoul set out to gather these records into one place and turn them into usable policy information.

**Policy content & implementation.** The city prepared an information-strategy plan in 2020 and, in December 2021, enacted the Ordinance on the Operation of Seoul's Building and Housing Comprehensive Information System — the first such legal basis among Korean local governments. The system expanded in stages through 2025: a third phase covered the full life cycle of a building from permit through commencement, completion, management, and demolition, and a fourth phase opened the data to citizens. By the ordinance's schedule it links some two dozen related systems — permitting, real-estate records, redevelopment, V-World, the city's integrated spatial data warehouse, and more. Its core aim is not merely to pool data but to process and analyse the integrated database to produce policy-grade information across housing supply, building safety, the housing safety net, after-care, and redevelopment.

**Outcomes & limitations.** On the strength of an ordinance, information dispersed across agencies was integrated in stages, building life-cycle information was placed under unified history management, and the fourth-phase public release raised transparency. Yet building-and-housing data are numerous and complex across the permit-to-demolition span, making upkeep difficult and leaving statistics vulnerable to error when managed in isolation — as when a 2024 national housing-supply tally omitted Seoul redevelopment volumes. Because much data is generated by central and district systems, Seoul-specific quality control is still required, and the system has not yet matured from a database into a true 'platform' that nurtures private information services.

**Implications for global and developing cities.** The essence here is 'producing policy information,' not merely 'integrating data.' A city should link scattered systems into one integrated database, but must design through to the stage of processing and analysing that database for decisions — and an institutional basis such as an ordinance is what determines the system's sustainability. Four supports must accompany the technology: a control tower with a long-term roadmap, collaboration with the private sector and academia, the digital competence of officials, and provisions against the digital divide and security risks. Without these, cloud-and-AI transition remains a procurement exercise rather than a capability.

content.en: The Urban Space Portal and the 'Urban Hub' Challenge

content.text: **Background & context.** Information such as use-zones, district-unit plans, and planning facilities is dense with technical terms and organised around legal notices, making it hard for citizens to understand. Separately, as the central government rolled out a standard UPIS nationwide and later a cloud-based integrated land-use platform (KLIP), the question arose of how a city with its own mature system should connect to national infrastructure without duplicating cost, losing history, or fragmenting its data.

**Policy content & implementation.** On the disclosure side, Seoul launched an urban-planning portal in 2011 — today's Urban Space Portal — with map services for planning facilities, district-unit plans, redevelopment areas, and height limits, plus an alert service that shifted citizens from 'coming to find' information to 'being told' about decisions in districts they follow. Guidelines revised in 2021 and 2023 added online collection of residents' opinions and required registration from the drafting stage, and a recent 'Seoul Plan+' integrated project information on a map. On governance, Seoul's strategy toward national platforms is to maintain consistency in the short term through dedicated data-inspection staff and real-time replication and API linkage, and over the longer term to evolve its system into a provisional 'Urban Hub' that defines the functions national platforms lack and integrates dispersed information for combined use.

**Outcomes & limitations.** Citizens can now check what is happening around their own living area rather than relying on notice-text searches, and the receipt, review, and reply history of residents' opinions can be recorded. Yet exposed technical terms still defeat non-experts, information is duplicated across several municipal sites, and the portal map is a non-binding reference. On governance, the challenge of a mature system is 'better connection,' not 'more functions': trying to merge every system invites bloat, so only what yields real integration benefit should be merged, with the rest linked through standardised APIs under a 'single-source-of-truth' principle — while managing the burden of parallel national and local systems, preserving Seoul-specific information, and ensuring the accountability of any AI introduced.

**Implications for global and developing cities.** Disclosure must move beyond 'releasing data' to 'comprehensible disclosure': what a citizen needs is not a list of notices but an explanation of 'what is happening in my living area' and 'when and how to comment,' and participation must be designed as a traceable procedure rather than a feature. For governance, divide the roles of central platform, local system, departmental systems, and citizen portal; connect them with APIs and a single-source-of-truth rule to avoid duplicate entry; and make data production and quality control a standing statutory duty. Responsibly combined generative AI can then translate technical disclosure into plain language and widen access dramatically.

Seoul's experience confirms a coherent set of principles. The information system must be designed across the three layers together: on the e-government foundation of institutions, cloud, and data governance, the UPIS processes planning work to produce trustworthy data, and the digital twin analyses that data in three-dimensional space. Building any one layer well does not make the whole work. The core is not a 'map service' but the life-cycle management of legally binding decision data — putting drafting, deliberation, decision, notice, disclosure, and update into one flow, with spatial data and original documents managed together.

Two further principles run through every case. A system is harder to 'maintain' than to 'build': production, processing, and quality control of data must be made a standing statutory duty, with dedicated staff and budget. And the task of a mature system is 'better connection,' not 'more functions': the roles of central platform, local system, departmental systems, and citizen portal must be divided and joined by APIs and a single-source-of-truth principle, with comprehensible disclosure and traceable participation as the measure of success.

For a growing city the timing argument is decisive. Resources and time are short — but building a standardised database and workflow before information scatters into paper and personal computers costs far less than integrating it late. Just as a plan shapes a city's form for a century, an information system shapes the skeleton of urban administration for decades. Good urban planning begins with good information.
1. Design the three layers as one connected system; the weakest layer caps the value of the whole.
2. Make the system manage legally binding decision data across their full life cycle, not merely display them on a map.
3. Treat maintenance as the hard problem: legislate data-quality work as a standing duty with dedicated staff and budget.
4. Pursue 'better connection,' not 'more functions': integrate only where it pays, and link the rest with APIs under a single-source-of-truth rule.
5. Make disclosure comprehensible and participation traceable, and build the database before information scatters.

chapters.label: 3장
chapters.partname: Part 1 · AI and Data Methods for Planning
chapters.titleEN: From Virtual Seoul to S-Map: The Urban Digital Twin

content.text: "A digital twin is not a beautiful 3D map; it is a place to ask the city questions it cannot answer in two dimensions."

Some of the most consequential facts about a city are invisible on a plan. Whether a new tower will cast a neighbouring school into shadow, whether a wall of buildings will choke the breeze that cools a summer street, whether a low-lying district will flood when a culvert is overwhelmed — these are three-dimensional, dynamic questions. A two-dimensional map can show what is where; only a three-dimensional model that reproduces the city's geometry and feeds on live data can show what effect a thing has on the space around it. That model is the urban digital twin, and building one at city scale is among the most ambitious instruments a planning administration can wield.

A digital twin is a virtual model that represents a physical object identically inside a computer, allowing real-world situations to be simulated, visualised, predicted, and optimised. Estimates of its economic value have been large — a widely cited projection put the value created by the Internet of Things and associated digital-twin technologies in the trillions of dollars, with a substantial share in cities — but the value is not automatic. It is realised only when the twin is kept current, connected to authoritative planning data, and pointed at decisions that matter. This chapter explains what a city-scale digital twin is, how Seoul built one in Virtual Seoul (S-Map), what it is used for, and the hard limits that separate a working instrument from an expensive ornament.

Seoul did not build in a vacuum. Governments abroad were already pursuing city and national twins: Singapore's Virtual Singapore, led from the Prime Minister's Office, projected an entire nation in 3D; the United Kingdom's Digital Built Britain pursued the federation of individual twins; and Korea's national programme built a nationwide 3D map under 'Digital Twin Korea,' with a spatial-information platform serving 2D and 3D maps through open APIs. Seoul's contribution is a high-precision city twin tightly fused with administration and citizen service — the most advanced expression of the three-layer integration set out in Chapter 2.

At its foundation, a city-scale digital twin is three things stacked together: a high-fidelity 3D representation of physical reality, a live connection to data about that reality, and an analytical engine that runs simulations on the model. The representation is built from a 3D spatial database — terrain, buildings, infrastructure, and, in advanced cases, indoor, underground, and below-ground utility space. The live connection draws in sensor, administrative, and situational data so the twin is not a frozen snapshot but a breathing model. The analytical engine is what distinguishes a twin from a 3D viewer: it computes sunlight hours, wind corridors, sight-lines, and flood propagation, turning geometry into insight.

The principle that justifies the investment is that the twin makes the invisible analysable. Phenomena that cannot be observed directly in reality — how a proposed development changes the wind field, how a building's height affects a protected view, how stormwater will spread across a basin — can be tested in the virtual space before a single decision is made in the physical one. This reframes the twin's purpose: it is not a visualisation deliverable but a decision-support instrument. The test of a twin is not how photorealistic it looks but how many real decisions it improves.

A second principle is that a twin is only as good as its inputs and its currency. The data flowing into a twin must be authoritative — which is precisely why the twin sits atop the UPIS and e-government layers of the previous chapter: the UPIS produces trustworthy planning data that becomes the twin's input, and the twin's analysis flows back as evidence for the next plan. Equally, a twin decays. A model that reflected the city accurately last year but has not been updated will produce analyses that are confidently wrong. Currency — continuous maintenance of the 3D base — is not a maintenance line-item; it is the precondition of the twin's validity.

A third principle concerns governance and the boundary between model and reality. A high-precision twin contains sensitive information — the location of underground utilities, the interiors of secure facilities — so disclosure must be balanced against security. And every twin embeds simplifying assumptions; its flood model, however sophisticated, is a model, not the flood. Responsible practice treats the twin's outputs as evidence to be interpreted by experts and ratified by public process, never as an oracle. The gap between the model and the world it represents is permanent, and good governance manages it openly rather than pretending it away.

Finally, a twin is a platform for participation, not only for analysis. When the 3D model is opened to citizens — to walk a proposed streetscape, to see a tower in its context, to comment in a shared virtual space — design review and public deliberation gain a common ground that words and 2D drawings cannot provide. Seoul's experiments with a metaverse civic space extend this logic, though, as with all the tools in this book, the technology widens the channel for participation without guaranteeing that participation is meaningful; that depends on how the process is designed.

Seoul's digital-twin journey runs from early experiments in extended-reality urban modelling, through the construction of the high-precision S-Map twin, to its integration into the city's 2024 smart-city master plan. Three cases show the arc: the foundational build of Virtual Seoul (S-Map); its operational use in environmental, planning, and safety analysis; and its extension into citizen participation and the metaverse.

**Background & context.** To address urban problems and prepare for an era of AI convergence, Seoul set out to build a 3D digital twin, Virtual Seoul (S-Map), that links real-time information — sensor, administrative, and situational — to a replica space identical to reality, supporting policy decisions through analysis and simulation. The ambition built on earlier extended-reality and 'robot city' research that had explored how to model the city in immersive 3D, and on Seoul's status as a repeated top performer in global e-government surveys, which gave it both the data assets and the institutional confidence to attempt a city-scale twin.

**Policy content & implementation.** An information-strategy plan was prepared in 2019–2020, and a set of just over a hundred standard map datasets — including indoor, outdoor, and underground spatial information — was selected and built in stages. The resulting platform is organised as four mutually reinforcing spaces: a civic-innovation space supporting urban-planning and landscape deliberation, fire safety, and wind-corridor administration; a citizen-participation space; an experiment-and-demonstration space (an OpenLab for industry-academia collaboration); and a data-hub space maintaining the currency of the 3D standard map and its linkage to related agencies. The twin was embedded into Seoul's broader smart-city architecture alongside the integrated TOPIS traffic system and the Smart Pole street infrastructure.

**Outcomes & limitations.** S-Map gave Seoul a high-precision 3D base on which administration and citizen service could be unified, and the OpenLab seeded an industry-academia ecosystem around it. The limitations are intrinsic to the technology. The twin's value depends on data currency, so continuous upkeep is mandatory; underground facilities and secure interiors require a careful balance of security against disclosure; and high-precision 3D construction is costly, so its worth must be proven through demonstrated contribution to real policy rather than asserted from its sophistication.

**Implications for global and developing cities.** The transferable architecture is the four-space design: build the 3D base as a shared data hub, then wrap it with administrative use, citizen participation, and an open experiment space so the twin is continuously exercised rather than admired. A developing city should resist building a city-wide high-precision twin all at once; the cost-and-currency burden is real. Starting with a priority district or a single analytical use, on an open national base map where one exists, is the disciplined path.

content.en: Simulation for Environment, Planning and Safety

content.text: **Background & context.** A 3D base is only justified by what it lets a city compute. Seoul's planning, environmental, and safety bureaus carried recurring questions — how development affects wind and sunlight, whether a tower intrudes on a protected view, where pedestrians and fire response are most vulnerable — that two-dimensional tools answered poorly or not at all. S-Map was built to answer exactly these.

**Policy content & implementation.** Representative uses span three domains. In the environmental domain, the twin runs wind-corridor simulation to predict the urban impact of development and to analyse the effect of new green space. In the urban-planning domain, it supports development and landscape deliberation directly: terrain sections, sunlight-rights analysis, viewpoint and sight-line studies, and height analysis are performed on the 3D model during committee review. In the safety domain, it supports the management of urban facilities, pedestrian-vulnerable areas, and fire response. This is the three-layer integration at its most advanced — the twin takes the UPIS's planning data as input and visualises and simulates its 'spatial impact.'

**Outcomes & limitations.** Use of S-Map in the city's urban and architecture deliberation committees, and by citizens, has risen markedly over recent years, indicating that the simulations are earning their place in real decisions rather than sitting unused. The limitation is that a simulation is an argument, not a verdict: a wind or flood model encodes assumptions that experts must interrogate, and its authority in a statutory deliberation derives from human review, not from the model itself. The twin sharpens the debate; it does not settle it.

**Implications for global and developing cities.** The lesson is to anchor a twin to a small number of high-value analytical questions where 3D genuinely beats 2D — sunlight, wind, view, flood — rather than to build capability in the abstract. These analyses also translate directly to other cities: a developing city facing heat and flooding can prioritise wind-corridor and stormwater simulation, which deliver visible public-health and disaster-risk benefits and justify the model's cost in terms a mayor and a resident can both understand.

**Background & context.** A twin that only officials use realises half its value. Seoul sought to open the 3D model to residents so that the spatial consequences of plans could be deliberated by the public, and to experiment with a virtual civic space — Metaverse Seoul — in which services and participation could occur inside the digital city itself.

**Policy content & implementation.** Within S-Map's citizen-participation space, residents can examine the spatial impact of plans on the same 3D base that officials use, and the platform supports citizen-facing policy participation rather than passive viewing. Metaverse Seoul extended this into an immersive environment for selected public services and civic interaction. Together they reposition the twin from an internal analytical tool to a shared ground for deliberation, where a proposed building or streetscape can be seen in context by anyone, lowering the expertise barrier that ordinarily excludes residents from technical design review.

**Outcomes & limitations.** Citizen use of S-Map's public services has grown alongside its administrative use, suggesting genuine appetite for spatial information presented in three dimensions. Yet the limits are familiar from earlier chapters: opening a channel does not guarantee meaningful participation, the metaverse layer must prove sustained civic value beyond novelty, and accessibility must be managed so the 3D interface does not simply relocate the digital divide into a more immersive form. The technology widens the door; the process design determines who actually walks through it.

**Implications for global and developing cities.** The durable lesson is that a digital twin is a participation infrastructure, not only an analytical one — but participation must be designed as a procedure, with feedback on how citizen input changed a decision, or the 3D experience becomes spectacle. A city need not build a metaverse to capture most of this value; an accessible, well-explained 3D viewer of proposed plans, tied to a real comment-and-response process, delivers the core benefit at a fraction of the cost.

Seoul's experience makes the digital twin's logic concrete. A digital twin is not a beautiful 3D map; its purpose is to support policy decisions. Its investment is justified when it analyses phenomena that are invisible in reality — wind, sunlight, view, flood — to provide grounds for planning, deliberation, and disaster response. And its value compounds when it connects to the UPIS and e-government layers beneath it: the UPIS's trustworthy data become the twin's input, and the twin's analysis returns as evidence for the next plan, completing a virtuous loop rather than standing as an isolated showpiece.

The honest counterweight is the set of hard limits. A twin's value rests on data currency, so continuous maintenance is not optional but constitutive. Underground and secure information demands a deliberate balance of disclosure against security. High-precision 3D is expensive, so the twin must earn its cost through demonstrated policy contribution, not asserted sophistication. And the gap between model and reality is permanent: the twin's outputs are evidence for human judgement, never a substitute for it.

For a growing city the sequencing is everything. Build the twin where it answers a question that matters, on an open base map where one exists, and exercise it continuously through administration, simulation, and participation. A twin built all at once and left to decay is the most expensive mistake in this book; a twin built incrementally around real decisions is among its best investments.
1. Build the twin as a decision-support instrument, not a visualisation deliverable; judge it by decisions improved, not by realism.
2. Anchor it to high-value 3D questions — sunlight, wind, view, flood — where three dimensions genuinely beat two.
3. Connect it to the UPIS and e-government layers so authoritative data feed the twin and its analysis feeds the next plan.
4. Treat currency and maintenance as constitutive, and balance disclosure of sensitive 3D data against security.
5. Use the twin as participation infrastructure, designing feedback into a real comment-and-response process; start with a district before attempting the whole city.

chapters.num: The 15-Minute City as a Data Problem
chapters.partname: Part 1 · AI and Data Methods for Planning
chapters.titleEN: The 15-Minute City as a Data Problem

content.text: "The 15-minute city is not a map of facilities; it is a measurement of lived time." The slogan that has travelled from Paris to Bogotá to Seoul promises something deceptively simple: that every resident should reach the essentials of daily life—work, school, groceries, green space, health care, and culture—within a short walk or cycle of home. Yet the moment a planner tries to act on that promise, the slogan dissolves into a series of hard, quantitative questions. Fifteen minutes for whom? Walking at what speed, along which streets, through which gradients and crossings? Reaching which destinations, weighted by what definition of "essential"? The 15-minute city is, before it is anything else, a data problem.

This chapter argues that the principal obstacle to operationalizing proximity is neither political will nor design imagination but the absence of an integrated, comparable, and context-sensitive evidence base. A city can declare a proximity target in a single sentence, but it cannot govern toward that target without a pipeline that ingests street networks, transit schedules, facility inventories, and population characteristics, and that returns accessibility, equity, and environmental indicators a planning committee can actually debate. Where that pipeline is missing, the 15-minute city becomes a branding exercise; where it exists, it becomes a steering instrument.

We develop this argument in two registers. The first is conceptual: how chrono-urbanism reframes the city as an organization of time rather than of land use, and why this reframing generates measurement obligations that traditional zoning never imposed. The second is institutional: how an international research consortium and two East Asian metropolitan administrations have tried to build the data infrastructure that proximity planning demands. The lesson for world cities and for developing-country administrations is that the 15-minute city is exportable as a method only to the degree that its data assumptions are made explicit, adaptable, and open.

The intellectual lineage of proximity planning runs from the neighbourhood unit of the early twentieth century through Jane Jacobs's defence of mixed, fine-grained urban fabric to Carlos Moreno's formalization of "chrono-urbanism" and the 15-minute city in the late 2010s. Moreno's contribution was to invert the planning question. Rather than asking how to move people efficiently across a functionally segregated city, he asked how to reorganize the city so that the need to move is reduced—measuring quality of life by the time-cost of reaching daily functions. This is a temporal rather than a spatial logic, and time, unlike land, is experienced unequally by every resident depending on age, mobility, income, and the body they inhabit.

The first principle, therefore, is that proximity is relational, not absolute. A 15-minute isochrone is meaningless until one specifies a traveller and a mode. A fit adult walking at 5 km/h covers roughly 1.2 kilometres in fifteen minutes; a frail older person, a parent with a stroller, or a wheelchair user may cover half that, and only where the footway is continuous and the crossings are safe. Melbourne's adoption of a 20-minute rather than 15-minute neighbourhood, and Bogotá's Barrios Vitales programme, are not merely local rebrandings but recognitions that the time budget must be calibrated to context, density, and the realistic speed of the most constrained user.

The second principle is that access must be measured on the real network, not as the crow flies. Euclidean buffers systematically overstate accessibility because they ignore rivers, rail lines, motorways, blocked footways, and the topography that make two points 400 metres apart a twenty-minute detour. Operational proximity analysis requires routing on an actual pedestrian and cycling graph—derived from OpenStreetMap or an official cadastre—and, for destinations beyond walking range, on scheduled public transport. The unit of analysis is not the administrative district but a fine grid, often 100 metres, that lets the analyst see how access varies block by block.

The third principle is that proximity is an equity question disguised as a logistics question. Counting facilities within a catchment is insufficient when those facilities are congested, capacity-constrained, or competed for by surrounding populations. A clinic 600 metres away that serves 40,000 people is not "accessible" in any meaningful sense. Robust accessibility metrics must therefore weight supply by demand and distance simultaneously, and must be disaggregated to reveal which neighbourhoods, and which demographic groups within them, fall below an acceptable threshold. The 15-minute city is a redistributive programme, and it can only be governed if its distributional effects are made visible.

The fourth principle is openness. Because proximity targets are politically contested—touching property values, retail location, school catchments, and transport investment—the evidence used to set and monitor them must be inspectable, reproducible, and transferable across the very different data environments of wealthy and developing cities. A proprietary black box that produces an accessibility score commands no democratic legitimacy. An open-source pipeline that any city can audit, recalibrate to local walking speeds, and rerun on its own data is both a scientific and a governance asset.

**Background & context.** In 2024 the Seoul Institute, the metropolitan government's policy research arm, coordinated a pre-proposal to the European "Driving Urban Transitions" (DUT) Partnership under its 15-Minute City transition pathway, specifically the strand on "data and indicators for effective decision-making." Titled "An Integrated Open-Source Pipeline for Data-Driven Decision-Making in 15-Minute City Planning Across Diverse Urban Contexts" (acronym Data2DecisionPath), the bid responded to a recurring frustration in proximity planning: that every city reinvents its accessibility analysis from scratch, on incompatible data, with results that cannot be compared or reused. The proposal was led by the Institute's director and principal investigator, J. Choi, with a planned 36-month programme.

**Policy content & implementation.** The consortium united six partners across three countries—the Seoul Institute and Gachon and Hanyang Universities in Korea, Delft University of Technology in the Netherlands, the Politecnico di Milano in Italy, with the Seoul Metropolitan Government as a cooperation partner—pooling expertise in transport modelling, geospatial data science, and accessibility analysis. Its design rested on three sequential objectives. First, a cross-sectoral urban data ecosystem: protocols for data collection, gap analysis, and the fusion of mobility, land-use, and environmental data from heterogeneous sources. Second, a suite of adaptable analytical frameworks—context-sensitive accessibility metrics, environmental-impact assessment, and social-equity analysis—explicitly designed to be recalibrated to different urban morphologies. Third, the translation of those analytics into a user-friendly open-source platform so that planning offices without large data-science teams could still run the analysis.

**Outcomes & limitations.** As a research-design case rather than a completed deployment, the value of Data2DecisionPath lies in what its architecture reveals about the data problem. The deliberate separation of an ingestion layer, an analytics layer, and a decision-support interface mirrors the three principles above: explicit data assumptions, context-sensitive metrics, and openness. The limitation is equally instructive. A six-partner, multi-country consortium drawn predominantly from high-income research environments must work hard to ensure its tools degrade gracefully where data are sparse—where there is no GTFS feed, no complete building register, no reliable footway inventory—which is precisely the condition of many of the cities that most need proximity planning.

**Implications for global and developing cities.** The consortium model demonstrates that the hard part of the 15-minute city is institutional plumbing, not slogans. For developing cities the actionable lesson is to invest first in a minimal but honest data ecosystem—an editable street graph, a facilities inventory, and a population grid—rather than in a polished dashboard built on unexamined assumptions. An open-source pipeline lowers the entry cost, but only if it is engineered to run on whatever data a city actually has, with its gaps declared rather than hidden.

**Background & context.** Korea arrived at proximity planning through its own vocabulary. Seoul's 2040 Plan introduced the "walking-everyday-zone" (보행일상권, bohaeng-ilsangkwon), a unit within which residents can meet daily needs on foot, and Busan and other metropolitan governments have pursued parallel living-zone concepts. The motivation was domestic—an ageing population, hollowing-out of older neighbourhoods, and a desire to move beyond car-oriented superblocks—but the concept maps almost exactly onto the international 15-minute-city discourse, giving Korean cities both a policy mandate and a measurement obligation.

**Policy content & implementation.** Translating the walking-everyday-zone from a planning slogan into delineated zones with assigned facility programmes required exactly the data pipeline this chapter describes: pedestrian routing on a fine grid, facility inventories by sector, and accessibility scoring that exposes underserved pockets. The Korean implementations layered big administrative data—floating population, card-transaction footfall, and public-facility registries—onto the street network so that proposed zones could be tested against observed daily-life patterns rather than drawn by administrative convenience. This is where the Seoul and Busan experience connects directly to the methodological chapter that follows, which details the community-detection, accessibility, and optimization methods used to operationalize these zones.

**Outcomes & limitations.** The walking-everyday-zone gave proximity planning statutory standing in Korea's largest cities, which is a substantial achievement: it converts a desirable idea into a target that plans must address. The limitation is that a zone boundary, once drawn, hardens. Daily life does not respect administrative lines; people shop, school their children, and seek care across boundaries, and a grid-based accessibility surface can show this fluidity better than a discrete zone can accommodate it. The honest reading is that zones are a useful communicative device layered on top of a continuous accessibility analysis, not a substitute for it.

**Implications for global and developing cities.** The Korean cases show the value of anchoring proximity in an existing statutory plan rather than importing a foreign label. A city that already has a master-planning instrument can graft a proximity target onto it and inherit its legal force. The transferable caution is to keep the underlying analysis continuous and data-driven even when the political output is a tidy set of zones, so that the boundaries can be revisited as data and daily-life patterns evolve.

Across the consortium design and the two East Asian deployments, a consistent picture emerges. The 15-minute city succeeds as policy in proportion to the seriousness of its data infrastructure. Cities that treat proximity as a communications campaign produce isochrone maps that flatter the centre and ignore the periphery; cities that treat it as a measurement programme can locate the specific blocks and specific populations that the promise of proximity has failed, and can direct investment accordingly.

The deeper synthesis is that proximity planning forces a city to make its value judgements explicit. Choosing a walking speed, a list of essential destinations, and an equity threshold is not a technical act but a political one, performed in the language of data. The contribution of an open, auditable pipeline is to surface these choices for democratic debate rather than to bury them in a proprietary score. The following principles distil the chapter's argument.
1. Define "15 minutes" for the most constrained traveller, not the average one; calibrate the time budget to local density and demography, as Melbourne and Bogotá did.
2. Measure access on the real pedestrian, cycling, and transit network on a fine grid, never as Euclidean distance from administrative centroids.
3. Weight accessibility by demand and capacity so that proximity is assessed as an equity outcome, disaggregated by neighbourhood and demographic group.
4. Build the data ecosystem before the dashboard, and design tools to degrade gracefully where feeds and registries are incomplete.
5. Keep the pipeline open-source and reproducible so that proximity targets are inspectable, transferable, and democratically legitimate.
