chapters.label: 10장
chapters.titleEN: Bilateral Research Diplomacy: The Seoul–Shanghai Network

content.text: "Between two peer megacities, technical cooperation can hold steady even when politics does not." The Seoul–Shanghai research plan rests on this proposition. Where the previous chapter built a network of many cities, this one examines the quieter, denser form of cooperation between exactly two—a bilateral research relationship deep enough to co-design studies, exchange researchers, and translate each other's policy work.

Seoul and Shanghai are unusually well matched. Both are dense East Asian megacities of roughly comparable scale, both run sophisticated municipal planning-research institutes, both are wrestling with the same near-future agenda: metropolitan-region restructuring, climate resilience, regeneration of ageing districts, smart infrastructure, and the application of AI and big data to urban policy. Complementarity, not similarity, is what makes the pairing productive—each city has solved some problems the other is still facing.

In late 2025 the Seoul Institute commissioned a short, focused study—"Building a Seoul–Shanghai Inter-City Policy-Cooperation Research Network"—to design the institutional and operational scaffolding for that relationship, anchored on an MOU with the Shanghai Urban Planning and Design Research Institute (SUPDRI). This chapter treats that planning study as a case in the craft of bilateral 'research diplomacy': how two municipal think tanks turn mutual interest into a standing, repeatable collaboration.

The honest framing acknowledges geopolitics. Seoul and Shanghai sit within a wider strategic environment that neither institute controls. The plan's response is not to ignore that environment but to design around it—choosing technical, mutually beneficial themes, keeping channels at the institute-to-institute level, and building a structure resilient enough to outlast individual officials and diplomatic weather.

Bilateral research cooperation differs in kind from multilateral networking. A network of fourteen cities optimizes for reach and shared voice; a bilateral relationship optimizes for depth. With only two partners, transaction costs fall, agendas can be tailored precisely to mutual interest, and joint work can reach a granularity—shared datasets, co-authored methods, reciprocal site visits—that large networks rarely achieve. The first principle, then, is to know which instrument fits the purpose: bilateral for depth, multilateral for breadth, and ideally the two reinforcing each other.

The second principle is complementarity over symmetry. Cooperation between peers is most productive when each partner brings something the other lacks. Seoul's strength in data-driven, AI-assisted planning methods meets Shanghai's strength in large-scale metropolitan-region planning and rapid implementation; each city's policy archive is a resource the other cannot generate. Designing the agenda around these complementarities—rather than around topics where both are already strong—is what turns an exchange of courtesies into an exchange of knowledge.

The third principle is to institutionalize the relationship beyond individuals. Bilateral cooperation is notoriously fragile: it often lives in the personal network of one or two officials and evaporates when they move on. The plan's countermeasure is structural—an MOU as the durable instrument, quarterly video conferences and an annual seminar as the operating rhythm, defined procedures for researcher exchange and short-term visiting research, and explicit provision for sharing and, with consent, translating each other's reports. Rhythm and procedure are what convert goodwill into a relationship that survives staff turnover.

The fourth principle is to manage geopolitics through technical cooperation. When the political relationship between two cities' nations is uncertain, the surest ground is shared technical problems whose solutions benefit both sides regardless of politics—climate adaptation, regeneration, infrastructure safety. Keeping cooperation at the institute-to-institute, researcher-to-researcher level, and keeping the agenda demonstrably technical and mutual, insulates the work from diplomatic volatility. This is not naïveté about politics; it is a deliberate choice of where to build so that the structure can stand in unsettled weather.

content.en: Standing Up the Seoul–Shanghai Network and the SUPDRI MOU

content.text: **Background & context.** As exchange among large cities intensified, the Seoul Institute judged that it needed a sustainable, institutionalized basis for international joint research rather than ad-hoc contacts. A two-month commissioned study (17 November–31 December 2025), led by researcher Choi Jun-young with a four-person team, was tasked with designing the cooperation system: how to make Seoul–Shanghai policy and spatial-planning research a continuing rather than episodic activity. The study's own framing positions it as a sibling of the MeTTA work of the same year—bilateral depth complementing multilateral breadth.

**Policy content & implementation.** The plan's core instrument is an MOU between the Seoul Institute and the Shanghai Urban Planning and Design Research Institute (SUPDRI). Around it, the study designs an operating system: quarterly video conferences and an annual seminar; defined procedures for researcher exchange, short-term visiting research, and jointly planned seminars and fora; mutual sharing of research reports and policy materials, with translation permitted by consent; and a delegation visit from the Seoul Institute to SUPDRI in Shanghai to launch the relationship in person. The forms of exchange are specified deliberately—academic conferences, short training stays, joint and visiting research—so that cooperation has concrete channels rather than only intentions.

**Outcomes & limitations.** Because the study is a forward-looking planning exercise completed at the end of 2025, its 'outcome' is the cooperation architecture itself: an MOU framework, a meeting rhythm, exchange procedures, and an agreed set of themes. Its limitations are equally clear. A two-month study with a modest budget can design the scaffold but cannot guarantee that SUPDRI will commit matching resources, that quarterly meetings will hold their cadence, or that joint studies will reach co-authored output. Like all bilateral plans, its durability will be tested by staff turnover and by the wider geopolitical environment it cannot control.

**Implications for global and developing cities.** The transferable lesson is that bilateral research diplomacy can be engineered deliberately: start with a single anchoring MOU between credible institutes; specify a meeting rhythm and concrete exchange procedures; provide for mutual translation and report-sharing; and launch with a reciprocal in-person visit. For developing cities, the model shows that a meaningful peer relationship need not be expensive—the binding constraint is not budget but the discipline to maintain rhythm and to keep the agenda genuinely mutual.

content.en: An Exemplary Joint Theme: AI and Big-Data Urban Policy Design

content.text: **Background & context.** The plan defines five joint themes: cross-border metropolitan-region linkage and the restructuring of wide-area spatial structure; climate-crisis-responsive urban and ecological planning and resilient territorial management; sustainable urban regeneration and community-centred 15-minute living zones; human-centred smart infrastructure and digital-based urban safety; and AI- and big-data-based urban policy design for citizens' quality of life. The last theme is the natural bridge to Part 1 of this book, where Seoul's living-zone analytics and open platforms were developed.

**Policy content & implementation.** Under this theme the cooperation would identify common research questions—how to design AI and big-data methods that fuse with urban and spatial policy, and how to implement them in two megacities with different data regimes—and pursue them through joint seminars, exchanged researchers, and shared methodological frameworks. Seoul's contribution draws directly on its data-driven planning toolchain; Shanghai contributes metropolitan-scale data and implementation experience. The plan explicitly frames AI/big-data and 15-minute-living-zone work as the leading edge where complementarity is greatest.

**Outcomes & limitations.** As an exemplar the AI/big-data theme shows the cooperation's logic clearly: a topic where Seoul leads on method, Shanghai leads on scale, and both gain from comparison. The limitation is that AI-and-data cooperation is the most data-sensitive of all the themes. Differences in data governance, privacy regimes, and what each city is permitted to share could narrow joint work to methods and aggregates rather than raw data. The plan's provision for consent-based sharing and translation anticipates this, but the practical ceiling on data-level collaboration is real and should not be understated.

**Implications for global and developing cities.** The theme demonstrates a reusable pattern for bilateral technical cooperation: pick a topic where the partners' strengths are complementary and the public benefit is unambiguous, then collaborate at the level of methods and shared frameworks where full data-sharing is constrained. For developing cities partnering with a more data-advanced peer, the lesson is to negotiate for method and capacity transfer—the parts that travel—rather than expecting access to the partner's proprietary datasets.

The Seoul–Shanghai plan is a compact demonstration that bilateral research diplomacy is a designable artifact. Its value lies less in any single theme than in the architecture connecting them: an anchoring MOU, a meeting rhythm, specified exchange procedures, and a set of complementary themes that give the two institutes durable reasons to keep talking. The plan does for two cities what the MeTTA reform does for many—converts relationship into instrument—at a depth that only a bilateral format allows.

The chapter's candid note is about fragility and geopolitics. Bilateral cooperation between cities embedded in a contested strategic environment cannot pretend that environment away. The plan's answer—technical themes, institute-level channels, structural rhythm—is a way of building cooperation that can withstand political weather, not a guarantee against it. Readers should weigh both the elegance of the design and the limits of what any institute-level plan can secure on its own.

Read alongside the previous chapter, the message is that breadth and depth are complementary instruments. A city think tank serious about international cooperation needs both a multilateral network for reach and shared voice, and a small number of deep bilateral relationships for the granular joint work that networks cannot sustain. Seoul is, deliberately, building both at once.
1. Choose the instrument to the purpose: bilateral for depth and granular joint work, multilateral for breadth and shared voice.
2. Build the agenda on complementarity—pair each partner's distinctive strength against the other's gap—rather than on topics where both already excel.
3. Institutionalize beyond individuals: anchor on an MOU, fix a meeting rhythm (quarterly calls, annual seminar), and specify exchange procedures so the relationship survives staff turnover.
4. Manage geopolitics through technical, mutually beneficial themes and institute-to-institute channels insulated from diplomatic volatility.
5. Where full data-sharing is constrained, collaborate at the level of methods and frameworks—and, for less-advanced partners, negotiate explicitly for method and capacity transfer.

chapters.label: 11장
chapters.titleEN: Open Geospatial Cooperation and Urban Diplomacy

content.text: "A donated dataset feeds a city for a year; a shared open-source toolchain feeds it for a generation." This adaptation of an old proverb frames the chapter's claim: in international urban cooperation, the most durable gift is not data or money but the open methods and free software with which a city can analyze itself—indefinitely, on its own terms.

Geospatial information is the substrate of modern planning. Where land is, who lives on it, how people move, where services are missing—these are spatial questions, and answering them requires both data and the software to work with it. Historically that software was expensive and proprietary, a barrier that fell hardest on the cities with the least money and the greatest need. The open-source geospatial movement—QGIS, PostGIS, the broader FOSS4G ecosystem—changed the economics, and with them the politics of who can plan with data.

This chapter argues that open data and open-source geospatial software are instruments of international cooperation in their own right: vehicles for capacity building, for what we will call geospatial diplomacy, and for a more equitable global geospatial-information agenda of the kind UN-GGIM articulates. The argument is grounded in the Seoul Institute's 2023 work on open-spatial-information-based international cooperation, in Seoul's engagement with FOSS4G-Asia and the draft 'Seoul Declaration,' and in the road toward FOSS4G 2026 in Hiroshima.

The chapter is also honest about the failure modes. Open tools are not automatically sustainable; data 'openness' raises real questions of sovereignty; and a toolchain donated without the capacity to maintain it becomes one more abandoned project. We treat these not as footnotes but as the central design problems of doing open cooperation well.

The foundational case for open geospatial cooperation is economic and ethical at once. Proprietary GIS licensing imposes recurring costs that scale poorly for resource-constrained cities, locking planning capacity behind a paywall. Open-source tools remove that barrier, but the deeper benefit is autonomy: a city running QGIS and PostGIS owns its analytical capacity outright, free from vendor dependence and license renewal. The first principle is therefore that the goal of cooperation is autonomy, not dependence—the recipient should end the engagement more self-sufficient, not more reliant.

The second principle concerns sustainability over donation. The graveyard of development cooperation is full of donated systems that died when the donor left—software no one could maintain, dashboards no one could update, data no one could refresh. Sustainability requires that cooperation transfer not just a tool but the capacity to run, adapt, and extend it: documentation, training, local technical community, and a toolchain built from components that remain freely available. A gift that cannot be maintained is a liability disguised as generosity.

The third principle is data sovereignty. 'Open' is not a single value; it must be balanced against a city's legitimate authority over data about its own land and people. Geospatial data can be sensitive—on security, property, and privacy grounds—and global cooperation must respect each partner's right to decide what is shared, at what resolution, and under what licence. The ethically sound position distinguishes open methods and open software, which travel freely, from open data, which travels only with the data-holder's consent. Conflating the two invites both over-sharing and resistance to cooperation.

The fourth principle frames all of this as diplomacy. Standards, declarations, and shared agendas are how a community of practice coordinates—and they are also how soft power operates. UN-GGIM (the UN Committee of Experts on Global Geospatial Information Management) provides the global frame; regional FOSS4G gatherings and instruments like a 'Seoul Declaration' are how a city contributes to and shapes that frame. Geospatial diplomacy means using technical leadership in open tools to build relationships, reputation, and influence—an honest naming of why a city invests in apparently altruistic capacity building.

**Background & context.** In 2023 the Seoul Institute undertook a study on open-spatial-information-based international cooperation, positioning Seoul not merely as a user of open-source geospatial tools but as a convener within the regional open-geospatial community. The work connected to FOSS4G-Asia—the Asian arm of the global Free and Open Source Software for Geospatial movement—and to the broader UN-GGIM agenda, reflecting a judgement that Seoul's accumulated open-data and open-tool experience was a cooperation asset worth projecting regionally.

**Policy content & implementation.** The centrepiece was a draft 'Seoul Declaration' tied to FOSS4G-Asia and supported by a UN-GGIM-oriented report—an attempt to articulate shared principles for open geospatial cooperation across Asian cities: open standards, capacity building, respect for data sovereignty, and sustained community. Seoul paired the declaration work with active participation in FOSS4G-Asia events of 2023 and 2024, contributing methods and convening discussion, and looking ahead to FOSS4G 2026 in Hiroshima as a venue to carry the agenda forward. The instrument is characteristic of geospatial diplomacy: not a binding treaty but a coordinating statement that gives a loose community shared language and direction.

**Outcomes & limitations.** A declaration's outcomes are diffuse by nature—it builds alignment and visibility rather than delivering a measurable project, and its influence depends on whether members subsequently act on it. Seoul gained standing as a regional node in the open-geospatial community and a platform from which to advance capacity-building cooperation. The candid limitation is that declarations are easy to sign and hard to enforce; without follow-through in joint projects and training, a 'Seoul Declaration' risks becoming one more well-intentioned text. Its value is realized only if it seeds concrete capacity-building work.

**Implications for global and developing cities.** The episode shows how a city can convert technical capacity into diplomatic standing through open, community-oriented instruments rather than top-down aid. For developing cities, regional FOSS4G gatherings and instruments like the Seoul Declaration are accessible entry points: participation costs little, the tools are free, and the community is genuinely open to newcomers. The caution is symmetrical—signing a declaration is not capacity; the real work is the training and joint projects it is meant to catalyze.

content.en: Exporting an Open-Source Planning Toolchain for Capacity Building

content.text: **Background & context.** Part 1 of this book described Seoul's data-driven planning methods—the living-zone analytics combining community detection, accessibility modelling, and optimization, delivered through an open Data2DecisionPath-style platform. The natural extension into international cooperation is to package these as a transferable, open-source toolchain that another city can run on its own data, turning Seoul's methods from a domestic asset into a shared public good.

**Policy content & implementation.** The toolchain rests on open foundations—QGIS and the FOSS4G stack for the geospatial layer, open analytical methods for accessibility and reallocation, and an open platform for delivery—so that no proprietary dependency blocks adoption. Capacity building, not deployment, is the operating model: training local planners and analysts to run and adapt the methods, documenting them transparently, and publishing the open methods of the AI living-zone work so they can be reproduced and modified rather than treated as a black box. The aim is for a partner city to leave the engagement able to maintain and extend the toolchain itself.

**Outcomes & limitations.** The exemplar's promise is leverage: a method developed once in Seoul can, in principle, serve many cities at near-zero marginal licensing cost, with each city owning its analytical capacity. The limitations are exactly those flagged in the principles. Sustainability depends on local technical community and maintenance capacity that vary widely; data sovereignty constrains what can be shared and at what resolution; and methods calibrated to Seoul's dense, data-rich environment may transfer imperfectly to cities with sparser data. Honest cooperation foregrounds these constraints and adapts the toolchain to local data realities rather than imposing a Seoul-shaped template.

**Implications for global and developing cities.** An open-source planning toolchain is one of the most genuinely useful things an advanced city think tank can offer the Global South, precisely because it builds autonomy rather than dependence. The transferable design rules: insist on fully open components so nothing breaks when funding ends; centre the engagement on training local people; publish methods openly so they can be audited and adapted; and respect the partner city's sovereignty over its own data throughout.

Open geospatial cooperation reframes what international urban assistance can be. The classic model moved money and finished systems from rich cities to poor ones, often leaving dependence in its wake. The open model moves methods, software, and skills—assets that the recipient can own, run, and extend indefinitely. Seoul's 2023 work, its FOSS4G-Asia engagement and draft Seoul Declaration, and its open planning toolchain together sketch this alternative: cooperation as capacity transfer rather than charity.

The chapter has resisted any techno-optimist gloss. Open tools are not self-sustaining; openness is in tension with data sovereignty; and a toolchain without local maintenance capacity is a liability. These are not reasons to avoid open cooperation but the design constraints that distinguish doing it well from doing it for show. A 'Seoul Declaration' that is signed and forgotten, or a toolchain donated and abandoned, would reproduce the very failures the open model exists to correct.

Named plainly, this is geospatial diplomacy: a city converting technical leadership in open tools into relationships, reputation, and influence within UN-GGIM and the regional FOSS4G community. There is nothing dishonest in this naming. Soft power earned by genuinely building others' capacity is the benign end of the influence spectrum—and it is sustainable precisely because the benefit to recipients is real.
1. Aim cooperation at autonomy, not dependence—the partner should finish more self-sufficient than it started.
2. Build on fully open components (QGIS, the FOSS4G stack, open methods) so nothing breaks when external funding ends.
3. Distinguish open methods and software, which travel freely, from open data, which moves only with the data-holder's consent—respect data sovereignty throughout.
4. Centre engagements on training and local technical community, not on delivering finished systems.
5. Use coordinating instruments—the Seoul Declaration, UN-GGIM, FOSS4G gatherings—to align a community, but measure them by the concrete capacity-building they catalyze.

content.text: "Projects end; institutions remember." The preceding chapters of Part 2 described a portfolio of international cooperation—autonomous-delivery-robot research with Canada, the policy archive opened to the world, the MeTTA network, the Seoul–Shanghai bilateral, open geospatial diplomacy. Each was valuable; each was also vulnerable to the same fate, dependence on the individuals who happened to drive it. This closing chapter asks how a city think tank turns a scattered portfolio into a standing capability.

The Seoul Institute's answer, taking shape for 2026, is a dedicated Global Research Cooperation Center: a permanent organizational home for international cooperation, charged with four functions—conducting joint research, running a global knowledge platform, delivering capacity building and training, and serving as secretariat for the networks and bilateral MOUs the Institute has built. The Center is the institutional expression of a simple conviction: that cooperation worth doing is worth doing permanently.

This is the synthesis chapter of Part 2 and the bridge to the book's conclusion. It draws the threads of Part 2 together and ties them back to Part 1, whose argument was that Seoul's AI- and data-driven planning methods are what make Seoul worth cooperating with in the first place. Method and cooperation are not separate stories: the Center exists to carry Seoul's methods outward and to bring the world's questions back in.

The chapter keeps a critical eye on its own subject. Creating a center is easy; making it matter is hard. An office can become a coordinating bottleneck, a place where cooperation is administered rather than enabled. The design question is how to build an institution that sustains cooperation beyond any individual without smothering the initiative that made cooperation worthwhile—and how to know, by honest metrics, whether it is succeeding.

The first principle is the move from project-based to institutionalized cooperation. Project-based cooperation is opportunistic and fragile: it follows funding cycles and personal networks, and it forgets. Institutionalized cooperation provides continuity—a stable home that accumulates relationships, methods, and memory across many projects, so that each new engagement starts from the capital of the last rather than from zero. The center is the vehicle for that accumulation; without it, a think tank repeatedly rebuilds the same partnerships from scratch.

The second principle is sustaining cooperation beyond individuals. The recurring vulnerability across Part 2 was personal dependence—relationships living in one researcher's contacts, dissolving when that person moved. Institutionalization is the structural cure: documented partnerships, standing secretariats, defined procedures, and onboarding that transfers relational knowledge to successors. The aim is a cooperation capacity that belongs to the institution, not to its current staff—durable precisely because it does not depend on any one person remaining.

The third principle is integration across functions. The four functions—joint research, knowledge platform, capacity building, and secretariat services—are not a menu but a system. Joint research generates knowledge; the platform disseminates it; capacity building transfers it; the secretariat sustains the networks through which all three flow. Designed together, they reinforce one another: a network the center serves as secretariat becomes the channel for its joint research and the audience for its platform and training. Designed in isolation, each function underperforms.

The fourth principle is honest metrics of success. International cooperation invites vanity measurement—number of MOUs signed, delegations hosted, events held—that rewards activity over outcome. A center serious about value must measure what actually changed: joint research published and used, partner cities whose capacity demonstrably grew, networks that produced shared knowledge, methods adopted abroad. Choosing outcome metrics over activity metrics is itself a governance decision, and a difficult one, because activity is easy to count and outcomes are not. Naming this tension is part of designing the institution well.

content.en: Establishing the Global Research Cooperation Center

content.text: **Background & context.** By the end of 2025 the Seoul Institute had assembled, across the chapters of Part 2, a substantial but dispersed international portfolio: the MeTTA secretariat, a new Seoul–Shanghai bilateral, partnership agreements with the Institut Paris Region and others, open geospatial cooperation, and an internationally opened policy archive. The Institute judged that this portfolio had outgrown ad-hoc management and drafted, in December 2025, an operating plan and concept for a 2026 Global Research Cooperation Center—framed in its own materials as a 'leap toward a global research hub.'

**Policy content & implementation.** The Center is designed around four functions. First, joint research: commissioning and co-producing international studies, including MeTTA's 'AI and Megacity Planning' theme and the Seoul–Shanghai joint themes. Second, a global knowledge platform: a standing channel to disseminate Seoul's methods and policy knowledge and to host shared resources. Third, capacity building and training: programmes that transfer Seoul's data-driven planning methods to partner and developing cities. Fourth, secretariat services: housing the continuity functions for MeTTA and for the Institute's bilateral MOUs in one place. Concentrating these in a dedicated center, rather than dispersing them across project teams, is the core institutional choice.

**Outcomes & limitations.** As a 2026 establishment plan, the Center's outcomes are prospective; what can be assessed now is the design. Its strength is that it directly addresses Part 2's recurring weakness—personal dependence and project fragility—by giving cooperation a permanent home with explicit memory and secretariat functions. Its risks are equally identifiable: a center can become a coordinating bottleneck; its success is hard to measure and easy to fake with activity metrics; and a permanent office requires permanent budget and political backing that must be defended each cycle. The plan's candour about being a 'draft' and 'concept' is appropriate—the hard work of staffing, funding, and proving value lies ahead.

**Implications for global and developing cities.** The transferable lesson is that international cooperation eventually requires a structural home if it is to outlast its founders. Any city think tank with a growing cooperation portfolio faces the same threshold and the same four-function template—research, platform, capacity building, secretariat. For developing-city institutes, the implication is twofold: a partner with such a center is a more reliable counterpart than one running cooperation off the side of a desk; and a modest version of the same structure, even one or two dedicated staff with a clear mandate, materially improves their own ability to sustain partnerships.

content.en: The Integrated Model: Methods + Network + Platform

content.text: **Background & context.** The Center's deeper rationale is integration. Seen separately, Seoul's analytical methods (Part 1) and its cooperation portfolio (Part 2) are two achievements; seen together, they are one engine. The methods are what make Seoul worth cooperating with; the networks are how cooperation reaches other cities; the platform is how knowledge circulates. The Center exists to fuse these into a single, self-reinforcing model rather than to administer them as separate programmes.

**Policy content & implementation.** In the integrated model, Seoul's data-driven methods feed the joint research; the joint research flows through MeTTA and the bilateral networks for which the Center is secretariat; the knowledge platform disseminates the results and the open toolchains; and capacity-building programmes transfer the methods to partner cities, who then contribute their own data and questions back into the next round of joint research. The open geospatial toolchain of the previous chapter is the connective material—open methods that can actually travel. Each function thus feeds the others in a loop, which is precisely what makes the model more than the sum of its parts.

**Outcomes & limitations.** The integrated model's promise is compounding returns: every cycle should leave Seoul's methods more tested, its networks denser, and its partners more capable. The limitation is that integration is demanding to operate—it requires the four functions to actually coordinate rather than to run as silos within one office, and it assumes a steady supply of methods worth sharing and partners worth sharing with. If any link weakens—methods stagnate, networks fray, the platform goes stale—the loop degrades into disconnected activities. Integration is an aspiration the Center must continually earn, not a property it acquires by being founded.

**Implications for global and developing cities.** The model clarifies what makes a city think tank a valuable cooperation partner: not its willingness to meet, but its possession of methods worth transferring and a structure for transferring them. For developing cities, this reframes the choice of partners around substance—seek counterparts whose methods you can actually adopt and who have a structure to help you adopt them. And it offers a growth path: today's recipient of capacity building can, by accumulating its own methods and partnerships, become tomorrow's contributor to the same loop.

The Global Research Cooperation Center is the institutional capstone of Part 2. It gathers the disparate threads—the Canada robot study, the global policy archive, MeTTA, Seoul–Shanghai, open geospatial diplomacy—into a single structure with memory, and it ties them back to Part 1 by making Seoul's methods the thing the cooperation exists to carry. The chapter's argument is that this move from portfolio to institution is not bureaucratic tidying but the precondition for cooperation that lasts.

The synthesis is also a warning held in tension with the promise. A center can sustain cooperation or it can ossify it; it can measure outcomes or flatter itself with activity; it can integrate functions or silo them. The difference lies in choices the founders make and the metrics they are honest enough to adopt. We have tried throughout Part 2 to model that honesty—naming the fragility, the data-sovereignty limits, the geopolitical exposure, the gap between declarations and follow-through—because an institution built on candour about its limits is sturdier than one built on its own press.

For policymakers and planners in world and developing cities, the closing message of Part 2 is this: cooperation is a capability to be built, not an event to be attended. Methods give a city something to offer; networks and bilaterals give it reach; an open platform lets knowledge circulate; and a dedicated, well-measured institution makes all of it durable. That is how a global city plans with AI not only for itself, but with—and for—the rest of the world.
1. Move from project-based to institutionalized cooperation: give the portfolio a permanent home that accumulates relationships, methods, and memory.
2. Design cooperation to survive its founders—standing secretariats, documented partnerships, and onboarding that transfers relational knowledge to successors.
3. Integrate the four functions—joint research, knowledge platform, capacity building, secretariat services—as one reinforcing system, not a menu of separate programmes.
4. Measure outcomes, not activity: joint research used, partner capacity grown, networks that produced shared knowledge, methods adopted abroad—and resist vanity metrics.
5. Anchor cooperation in substance: a city is worth partnering with when it has methods worth transferring and a structure for transferring them—Part 1 and Part 2 are one argument.

content.text: We opened this book with a planner who still needs a pencil, a map, and a meeting room. We close it convinced that she will keep all three — and that the most important thing the new instruments change is not the pencil but the room: who is in it, what evidence sits on the table, and how far beyond the city's borders the conversation now reaches.

The chapters between these covers traced two transitions through the concrete work of one institution. On the technological side, we watched planning move from digitized paperwork toward big data, machine learning, digital twins, and AI agents. On the relational side, we watched international urban cooperation shift from episodic visits toward standing, research-driven partnership. The point of telling both stories in one volume was to show that they are a single story: a global city that learns to plan with data is also a city that must learn to cooperate differently, because method only earns its keep when it travels.

What follows is not a summary so much as an accounting. We ask what the Seoul Institute's 2023–2026 experiments actually taught, what principles a planning institution might carry forward, and — with equal seriousness — what remains unsolved. A useful book for other cities ends not with a victory lap but with a ledger they can act on.

The first lesson from Part 1 is that data methods deliver most when they are aimed at a well-posed planning question, not adopted for their own sake. The living-zone work succeeded because it began with a recognizable problem — where should public services sit so that real communities can reach them — and only then reached for Louvain community detection, E2SFCA accessibility, and optimization. The fifteen-minute city became tractable for the same reason: it was reframed from a slogan into a measurable accessibility problem. Across the chapters, the projects that produced usable results were those where the model served a decision a planner already had to make.

The second lesson is that infrastructure is destiny. None of Seoul's recent AI work would have been possible without two decades of unglamorous investment in planning information systems, e-government, open data, and the 3D city models that matured into S-Map. Digital twins and AI agents are the visible frontier, but they stand on a foundation of clean data, shared standards, and institutional habits of digitization. Cities hoping to leap directly to the frontier should read Part 1 as a warning as much as an invitation: the substrate cannot be skipped.

The third lesson, drawn from the citizen-persona and AI-agent experiments, is that these tools are most honest when used to widen deliberation rather than to replace it. Simulated residents can surface objections and edge cases a planning team might miss, and can stress-test a policy before it meets the public. They cannot confer legitimacy. The value we observed was diagnostic — a way to ask better questions of a real community — not a substitute for asking the community itself.

Part 2 taught a complementary set of lessons about cooperation. The Korea–Canada sidewalk-robot study, the globalized Seoul Policy Archive, the multilateral MeTTA network, and the Seoul–Shanghai partnership all converged on one finding: durable cooperation is built on shared artifacts, not shared sentiment. When partners exchange a working model, a documented data schema, or an executable piece of the policy archive, the relationship survives staff turnover and political weather. When they exchange only goodwill and photo opportunities, it does not. The relational transition, in other words, is itself a data problem — about making knowledge portable enough to outlast the people who first carried it.

content.en: Toward an AI-Augmented, Networked Planning Institute

content.text: If the two transitions are real, the institution that plans a global city must change shape. It becomes neither a pure technology shop nor a diplomatic secretariat, but something new: an AI-augmented, networked planning institute. From the experience reported in this book, we distill five principles for building one.

1. Start from the planning question, not the technique. Let the decision to be made select the method; treat every model as a means to a public end, and be willing to use the simplest tool that answers the question honestly.

2. Invest in the substrate before the frontier. Open data, common standards, a maintained digital twin, and disciplined data governance are the preconditions for AI in planning — fund them as long-term public infrastructure, not as project line-items.

3. Keep the human planner central and accountable. Use AI to augment judgment, widen deliberation, and surface what human teams overlook — never to launder a decision through a model that no one can explain to a citizen.

4. Cooperate through artifacts, not occasions. Make models, schemas, and executable knowledge the currency of international partnership so that cooperation produces something reusable and survives the departure of any individual.
5. Report the failures. An institution that publishes its limits honestly becomes more useful to its peers and more trusted at home; candor is a competitive advantage, not a liability.

Several problems remain stubbornly open, and we would be writing a brochure rather than a book if we hid them. The first cluster concerns data governance and equity. Data-driven planning is only as fair as its data, and the populations least visible in administrative and sensor data — informal workers, undocumented residents, the very old, the very poor — risk being optimized around rather than for. A digital divide can quietly become a planning divide if accessibility models are calibrated on the connected and the counted. Strong governance over what data is collected, how it is used, and who can audit the models is not a compliance afterthought; it is the precondition for any of these methods to serve the whole city.

The second cluster concerns the human planner and the durability of cooperation. There is a constant temptation to let a model's output substitute for contested public choice, eroding the planner's accountability until no one can say why a decision was made. Keeping the human central requires deliberate institutional design, not good intentions. Cooperation faces the mirror-image risk: too much of the international partnership documented here still depends on a handful of committed individuals and the relationships they personally hold. Institutionalizing those ties — so that a network like MeTTA or the Seoul–Shanghai partnership outlives the careers that built it — is the unfinished work that Chapter 12 names and that the field as a whole has yet to solve.

And yet the direction of travel is clear. The instruments will keep improving, the data will keep deepening, and the megacities of the coming decades will face shared problems — climate adaptation, ageing, mobility transitions, housing — that no city can model or solve alone. A planning institute that is at once AI-augmented and genuinely networked is, on the evidence of these pages, not a utopian ideal but an achievable next step. Seoul has taken a few of those steps and stumbled on some of them in full view. If this book helps another city take its own steps more surely, and to cooperate so that the stumbles need only happen once, it will have done its work.
